coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence switch sensor used for D-Penicillamine sensing and logic gate based on the fluorescence recovery of carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Yusheng
Zhao, Xin
Liu, Shaopu
Li, Yuanfang
Shi, Ying
Yan, Jingjing
Hu, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: A specific and sensitive fluorometric method based on the Carbon dots (CDs) probe which show unique optical properties is proposed for the determination of D-Penicillamine (D-PA) in human serum samples with satisfactory results. The fluorescence of CDs decreased apparently in the presence of mercury ion (Hg2+), Similarly, the addition of D-Penicillamine (D-PA) resulted in efficient fluorescence recovery of the CDs due to the strong affinity between Hg2+ and D-PA. Herein, this phenomenon has been exploited to design an “AND” logic gate and specific fluorescence switch sensor for sensitively sensing D-PA for the first time. Furthermore, the CDs possesses bright blue fluorescence with the maximum excitation and emission wavelength at 358nm and 442nm, respectively. Thus, after the experimental conditions are optimized, the linear range for detection PA is 2.0–24.0 μmolL−1, with correlation coefficient and detection limit is R2 =0.998, 0.6 μmolL−1, respectively. Moreover, the proposed method is not only expected to be come a potential tool for the fast response of D-PA but also possesses the potential for practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence recovery
D-Penicillamine
Logic gate
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Recommended procedure for determination of D-Penicillamine
Mechanism for the recognition of D-PA
Determination of D-PA in human plasma samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the detection of D-PA by CDs fluorescence probe.
(a) TEM image and (b) the corresponding size distribution histograms of the CDs.
FT-IR (a), Raman (b) and XRD (c) spectrum of the fluorescent CDs.
Characteristic optical spectra of CDs. An overlapping of absorption (a), excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra of CDs in aqueous solutions.
Effects of the D-PA on the fluorescence spectra of CDs- Hg2+ system. c(Hg2+): 4×10−5 molL−1; a–f c(D-PA): 0.9; 1.2; 1.5; 1.8; 2.1; 2.4×10−5 molL−1. pH=7.5.
Effects of acidity. c(Hg2+): 4×10−5 molL−1; c(D-PA): 21.0 μmolL−1.
Effect of concentration of Hg2+. pH=7.5.
Effect of ionic strength. c(Hg2+): 4×10−5 molL−1; c(D-PA): 21.0 μmolL−1; pH=7.5.
Fluorescence emission decay curves of CDs (a) and CDs–Hg2+ system (b). c(Hg2+): 4×10−5 molL−1. pH=7.5.
Absorption spectra of CDs–Hg2+–D-PA system. a: CDs, b: CDs–Hg2+, c: CDs–Hg2+–D-PA. c(Hg2+): 4×10−5 molL−1. c(D-PA): 21.0 μmolL−1 pH=7.5.
Calibration curve for D-PA. c(Hg2+): 4×10−5 molL−1. c(D-PA): 0.9; 1.2; 1.5; 1.8; 2.1; 2.4×10−5 molL−1. pH=7.5.
Truth table for the “AND” logic gate.
Analytical features of some typical methods employed for D-PA determination.

row.entry: Rapid, effective, and selective, but the sensitivity is lower
apply to liquid and solid samples analysis but make analysis cumbersome
Simultaneous multi-component detection and possesses high separation efficiency, yet equipments are expensive
effective, and selective but reagents is expensive
complicated steps and reagents are toxic
Effective and selective, but need expensive equipments

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Me-βCD-FL*: methyl–β-cyclodextrin–fluorescamine; VPG*: on-line system with vapor phase generation and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometric; HPLC-EC*: high performance liquid chromatography-electrochemical; AuNCs*: gold nanoclusters; CdS SQDs*: CdS semiconductor quantum dots; CMQT-derivatization*: 2-chloro-1-methylquinolinium tetrafluoroborate derivatization; CNDs*: carbon nanodots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of D-PA in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence switch sensor used for D-Penicillamine sensing and logic gate based on the fluorescence recovery of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time analytical Chemistry (Southwest University)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An “AND” logic gate and specific fluorescence switch sensor was designed for D-Penicillamine (D-PA).

A specific and sensitive fluorometric method based on the Carbon dots (CDs) probe which show unique optical properties is proposed for the determination of D-Penicillamine (D-PA) in human serum samples with satisfactory results. The fluorescence of CDs decreased apparently in the presence of mercury ion (Hg2+), Similarly, the addition of D-Penicillamine (D-PA) resulted in efficient fluorescence recovery of the CDs due to the strong affinity between Hg2+ and D-PA. Herein, this phenomenon has been exploited to design an “AND” logic gate and specific fluorescence switch sensor for sensitively sensing D-PA for the first time. Furthermore, the CDs possesses bright blue fluorescence with the maximum excitation and emission wavelength at 358nm and 442nm, respectively. Thus, after the experimental conditions are optimized, the linear range for detection PA is 2.0–24.0 μmolL−1, with correlation coefficient and detection limit is R2 =0.998, 0.6 μmolL−1, respectively. Moreover, the proposed method is not only expected to be come a potential tool for the fast response of D-PA but also possesses the potential for practical application.

ce:section.ce:para: D-Penicillamine (D-PA), 3-mercapto-d-valine, is the characteristic acid degradation product of β-lactam antibiotics. Penicillamine, which contains D- and L-enantiomeric forms show different biological and toxicological properties. However, only the D-enantiomer is clinically useful because of excessive toxicity of the L-type [1]. Thus, PA is the drug of choice not only used in the treatment of hepatolenticular degeneration (Wilson’s disease) but also for the treatment of several disorders including rheumatoid arthritis, primary biliay cirrhosis, scleroderma, fibrotic lung diseases, cystinuria, heavy element poisoning and progressive systemic sclerosis [2]. Recently, it has been used for the identify and quantify D-PA in skin samples from patients who had received this drug in a long term treatment and showed typical features of D-PA-induced elastosis [3].

Currently, It is important to quantitatively determine of D-PA in pharmaceuticals and biomedical samples. Up to now, the various analytical methods have been reported which include high performance liquid chromatography [4–6], fluorimetry [7,8], spectrophotometry [9], chemiluminescence [10], capillary electrophoresis [11–13], electrochemistry [14], voltammetry/electrochemical sensor [15–17] and kinetic assay [18] flow injection analysis [19] for the determination of D-PA in both pharmaceuticals and biomedical samples. Among these methods, Some of them have high sensitivities for the determination of D-PA, but it needed sophisticated and time consuming techniques, expensive instruments, long operation time, reagents that are mostly not available in many laboratories and low selectivity with higher interference problems. Furthermore, the important limitations for LC techniques is the fact that this thiol compound lacks sufficient UV absorption so a pre- or post-column derivatization procedure is normally required [20]. While the electrochemical methods are toxic for the mercury electrode which could cause environmental pollution and adverse effects to human beings. Though some of these methods use a modified electrode to replace the mercury electrode to the analysis, the modifications make the analysis cumbersome [21]. Herein, it is a highlight to develop an eco-friendly, sensitive, simple and selective method for determination of D-penicillamine in both pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples.

Recently, a new type of visible substitute, carbon dots (CDs) which are carbon-based materials, have recently been given considerably intense interest since they were first discovered during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [22]. CDs not only exhibit many fascinating optical properties, such as photoluminescence, photoinduced electron transfer, and electrochemiluminescence (ECL), but also present some additional advantages over the heavy metal-containing semiconductor-based CDs, such as chemical inertness, biocompatibility and low toxicity [23]. Therefore, CDs may become very promising substitutes of semiconductor-based CDs, and have attracted more and more attention in the past seven years [23–37]. Till now, the synthetic methods of CDs have become attractively and they can be classified into two types, for instance, “top-down” methods [24–32] and “bottom-up” approaches [33–36]. The top-down methods prepare CDs from larger carbon materials, such as nanodiamonds [24], graphite [25–28], carbon nanotubes [22,29], carbon soots [30], activate carbon [31], graphite oxide [32], while the bottom-up approaches synthesize CDs from molecular precursors such as citric acid [33], glucose [34,35], and resin [36]. Recently, CDs have received intensive research owing to their unique optical properties and their potential applications, especially in bioimaging [25,37,38] biosensing [39–41] and optoelectronic devices [42]. However, some of these methods usually involve complex processes, or need expensive starting materials and severe synthetic conditions, which limit the application of CDs in analytical chemistry. Therefore, approachable and suitable synthesis method is still urgently in demand for an ordinary laboratory.

Herein, the CDs that we synthesized are carbon nanodots due to their no quantum confinement, excitation-independent emission, broad PL band and short lifetimes [43]. A one-step pyrolysis method has been developed and has been utilized as an “off-on” fluorescence probe for the detection of D-PA. The simple detection strategy is presented in Scheme 1 . The sensing principle based on the fluorescence quenching of CDs by metal ions is presumably due to the non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation through effective electron transfer process [44]. The quenching mechanism of Hg2+ may be complex, both electron transfer and ion-binding, but the former effect may play a leading role [45]. When the D-PA occurs, due to its thiol group, which makes D-PA have the strong binding preference toward Hg2+ by forming Hg2+−S bond [44,46]. When Hg2+ was removed from the surface of CDs, the fluorescence of CDs recovered, therefore, the system thus could be turn on. Similarly, the fluorescence recovery intensity of CDs is in linear relationship with the concentration of D-PA, which makes it possible to detect D-PA. The method shows the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity and easy operation. Furthermore, the preparation of C-dots without the use of toxic raw materials, makes the probe more eco-friendly, which was pivotal for practical applications. What’s more, versatile logic gates with different functions were constructed. Most important, the proposed method has great potential application in the detection of D-PA in human serum samples with satisfactory results.

The fluorescence spectrum was recorded with an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) using a 1cm path length. A UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used for acquiring absorption spectra and measure absorbance. A high resolution transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company, USA) was used to characterize the morphology of the CDs, which was operated at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR-8400S, Kyoto, Japan) was employed to identify functional groups of the as-prepared CDs. A FL-TCSPC Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France) was used to measure fluorescence emission decay curves of systems. A pHS-3D pH meter (Shanghai Scientific Instruments Company, China) was used to measure the pH values.

The sodium citrate was purchased from Jinshan Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China), D-Penicillamine was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), NH4HCO3 and HgCl2 were purchased from Kelon Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). The Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer solutions at different pH values were prepared by mixing the mixed acid (composed of 2.71mL 85% H3PO4, 2.36mL HAc and 2.47g H3BO3) with 0.2molL−1 NaOH in different proportions. The buffer solutions were used to control the acidity. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification, and doubly distilled water was used throughout.

The pristine CDs was fabricated according to a hydrothermal method [47]. In general, 0.2g sodium citrate, 1.5g NH4HCO3 and 10mL double-distilled water were put into a Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave, respectively. Furthermore, the mixture was hydrothermally treated for 4h at 180°C in a drying oven. After cooling to room temperature, the transparent product was subjected to dialysis (1000Da, molecular weight cut off) against ultrapure water for 12h in order to obtain pure CDs. The purified homogeneous CDs were thus obtained, which were stable for several months. The final product was re-dissolved to 250mL with double-distilled water and stored at 4°C for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recommended procedure for determination of D-Penicillamine
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for D-Penicillamine (D-PA) detection is described as follows: Into a 10.0mL colorimetric tube, 0.5mL CDs (50mg/mL), 0.5mL Hg2+ (with a final concentration of Hg2+ 4×10−5 molL−1) and different amounts of D-PA (9.0; 12.0; 15.0; 18.0; 21.0; 24.0 μmolL−1) were added, and diluted into the mask with double-distilled water. Fluorescence spectra were measured at room temperature with excitation and emission at 358nm, 442nm, respectively. Both excitation and emission slit width were 10nm.

In order to demonstrate the practical utility of the new assay, the human plasma samples, obtained from different healthy people in local hospital, were selected for investigation. The analysis of real samples with 500 times diluted was performed. 0.1mL of the sample was added into a 10.0mL calibrated tube and diluted mask with ultrapure water. The resultant samples were spiked with standard D-PA solution at different concentration levels (1.0, 1.3, 1.6 μmolL−1) and then analyzed with the proposed method.

In order to characterize the as-prepared CDs, several instruments such as fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and FT-IR spectra were used to evaluate its characterizes from different aspects. Fig. 1 showed the morphology and the diameter distribution of the carbon dots. It clearly revealed that the as-synthesized CDs are spherical in shape (Fig. 1a) and the size distribution range between 2nm to 8nm with the average diameter about 5nm (Fig. 1b), which indicated that a one-pot facile synthetic strategy was established for the fabrication of CDs.

In order to provide further evidence for the components and the surface functional groups of the as-prepared CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) was measured to provide further evidence for the components and the surface functional groups of the as-prepared CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the absorption bands around 3402cm−1 was accounted for the stretching vibrations of OH and at 2929cm−1 related to the stretching vibration of CH. The peak at 1584cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration CC and CO stretching vibrations, while CN stretching vibrations at 1412cm−1, and 949cm−1 attributed to the bending vibrations of CH. Peaks at 1189 and 728cm−1 related to the stretching vibration of CO and NH, respectively. While the Raman spectra, shown in Fig. 2b, the peaks of CO, CN, CC/CO were obvious, which were attributed to the D band (sp3-hybridized) and G band (sp2-hybridized) of CDs, respectively. The D band was associated with the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered CDs. The G band corresponded to the E2g mode of CDs and was related to the vibration of the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. The coexistence of both bands indicated that the C-dots are amorphous [48,49]. Both of the FT-IR and Raman spectra revealed that multiple functional groups like COOH and a small amount of N-containing groups were presented on the surface of the synthesized CDs, which reveal that the as-prepared CDs were mainly surrounded by carboxylate and hydroxyl groups [50]. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CDs illustrated that the diffraction peaks were separately at 2θ =30.83°, 32.76° which could be indexed to the (1 0), (1 1) planes of amorphous carbon card (Fig. 2c)

The UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum (Fig. 3 ) of CDs were investigated when it came to the optical properties of CDs. Clearly, it emitted fluorescence as it possessed conjugated functional group. As shown in Fig. 3, the maximal absorption of CDs was at 340nm and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs showed a 442nm emission peak under the excitation of 358nm, with a Stokes shift of 102nm. The sodium citrate solution itself was non-emissive when excited at 358nm, which indicated that the bright blue fluorescence came from CDs. When excited at 358nm, the full width at half maximum of CDs emission spectrum was about 65nm, which was narrower than those reported (70nm). The quantum yield (Φ u) of CDs was calculated by using quinine sulfate (Φ s =0.55) as the reference standard [51].
Φ u
=
Φ s
·
F u
F s
·
A s
A u
where F u and F s referred to the areas under the fluorescence curves of CDs and standard, respectively. A u and A s were absorbance of the sample and standard at the excitation wavelength, respectively. The Φ u value of CDs was found to be 70%.

When D-PA was added to CDs-Hg2+ system, the fluorescence of the system could be recovered due to the competition reaction between Hg2+ and D-PA, the probe was thus “turn-on”. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the analytical system was gradually enhanced with the increase of the concentration of D-PA (Fig. 4 ). Therefore, the CDs-Hg2+ system can be proposed for the determination of D-PA.

Fig. 5 shows the influence of the solution acidity on the fluorescence intensities of the system with BR buffer, which pH ranging from 5.0 to 8.5. It could be found that the enhanced intensities reached the highest and remained constant at pH range of 7.3–7.7 by keeping the CDs and Hg2+ concentrations constant. As shown in Fig. 5, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs, CDs−Hg2+−D-PA respectively, while ΔF = F0 −F. When the acidity was lower than the optimum range, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually, while it reached higher, the quenching effect of Hg2+ decreased. Therefore, subsequent studies were performed at pH=7.5.

In order to evaluate the effect of concentration of Hg2+, different concentrations of Hg2+ were added to the aqueous solution of CDs and the fluorescence emission intensity was measured to study the sensitivity of the as-prepared CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 , There was a phenomenon that Hg2+ could quench of the fluorescence of CDs and similarly with the increase of the concentration of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased gradually. The fluorescence of the CDs could be almost quenched when the concentration of Hg2+ was 4×10−5 molL−1. Therefore, the concentration of 4×10−5 molL−1 Hg2+ was chosen as the optimum concentration for further study.

The effects of ionic strength were examined by adding different concentrations of NaCl solution while keeping the CDs, Hg2+ and D-PA concentrations and the pH constant (Fig. 7 ). The experimental results showed that the ΔF kept constant when the ionic strength was lower than 0.02molL−1. However the ΔF decreased gradually with the increasing of the concentration of NaCl. Therefore, the ionic intensity should be controlled and high concentration electrolyte should be avoided carefully in practical operation.

Therefore, To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, a tolerable error within±5.0%, the interference of foreign substances were evaluated. As shown in Table 1 , the effects of the inorganics (NaCl, ZnCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2, CdCl2, NH4Fe(SO4)2), vitamins (ascorbic acid, pyridoxine), amine acids (glycine, aspartic acid, arginine), carbohydrates (glucose, sucrose) were negligible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the recognition of D-PA
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the mechanism of the detection of D-PA using CDs as fluorescent probes. Initially, when CDs were free in aqueous solution, they showed strong fluorescence intensity. However, in the presence of Hg2+, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched significantly, The results confirm that Hg2+ induced-fluorescence quenching of CDs is ascribed to the close proximity of CDs induced by the chelation of Hg2+ with the carboxylate and hydroxyl groups of CDs, which changes the electronic structure of CDs and affect the distribution of excitons, thus facilitating the non-radiative recombination of excitons through the charge transfer process and leading to the fluorescence quenching of CDs [47]. One key feature of D-PA lies in its thiol group, which makes D-PA have the strong binding preference toward Hg2+ by forming Hg2+ S bond. Upon the addition of D-PA, the competitive interaction between Hg2+, D-PA, and CDs disturbs the interaction between Hg2+ and CDs. Hg2+ was then able to be removed from the surface of CDs, and thus the fluorescence of the system could be recovered. The different CDs fluorescent responses upon the addition of Hg2+ and D-PA make it possible to be used for D-PA detection.

Therefore, In order to clarify the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the interaction of D-PA with CDs was studied by spectrofluorometry at two temperatures (273K, 293K) (Fig. 7) with the Stern–Volmer equation [52,53]:
F 0
/ F = 1 + K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
= 1 + Ksv
[ Q ]
where F and F 0 represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching rate constant, which is a quenching efficiency measurement, and [Q] is the concentration of quencher. τ0 is the life time of fluorophore (taken as 10−8 s), Kq is quenching rate constants, and it could be calculated by the equation Kq= Ksv/τ0.

Fig. 7 shows the Stern–Volmer plots of F 0/F versus [Q] at two different temperatures, from which can be seen that with the rising of temperature, the quenching constant decreases. It can be concluded that the quenching process is probably by static quenching resulting from the formation of CDs-Hg2+ complex. Upon the addition of D-PA, the competitive interaction between Hg2+, D-PA, and CDs disturbs the interaction between Hg2+ and CDs. Hg2+ was then removed from the surface of CDs by D-PA lies in its thiol group by forming Hg2+ S bond, thus the fluorescence of the system could be recovered. In order to verify the real quenching mechanism further. Thus the fluorescence lifetime and UV–vis absorption spectra are employed to give some more evidence for the mechanism.

The powerful method to distinguish whether it is dynamic or static quenching mechanism is fluorescence lifetime. If dynamic quenching, τ 0/τ = F 0/F, while for the static quenching, τ 0/τ =1, the quenching medium can’t change fluorescence lifetime of excitation state fluorescence molecule [54]. Fluorescence emission decay curves of CDs and CDs-Hg2+ are performed on a FL-TCSPC Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 8 . the fluorescence lifetime of these two systems are 1.16ns and 1.40ns, respectively, which demonstrates the quenching of fluorescence of CDs by Hg2+ is a static quenching process.

Meanwhile, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+ were recorded. As shown in Fig. 9 , the absorption spectra of CDs (curve a), was quite different from the spectra of CDs–Hg2+ (curve b), which indicated that Hg2+ attached to the surface of CDs. Furthermore, when D-PA was added, the absorption spectrum (curve c) changed. This might because the binding force between CDs and Hg2+ was weak, while due to the thiol group of D-PA, which makes D-PA have the strong binding preference toward Hg2+ by forming Hg2+ S bond. In conclusion, the formation of these binary and ternary complexes could cause absorption spectral changes to different extents [55,56].

Under the optimum conditions, the linear range for the determination of D-PA was evaluated by fluorescence quenching method. The quenching efficiency (ΔF), however, displayed a good linear relationship with the concentration of D-PA in the range from 2.0 to 24.0 μmolL−1 (R2 =0.997) with the detection limit, which was 0.6 μmolL−1 (3σ/k). As shown in Fig. 10 , the fluorescence enhancement can also be described by the following equations: ΔF =415.51 c − 328.56, (ΔF = F−F 0, where F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensity of the CDs–Hg2+ system in the presence and absence of D-PA, respectively). Obviously, the fluorescence quenching method is much more sensitive fast, facile than the previous works, which were shown in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of D-PA in human plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para: The operating applicability of the proposed sensing system in real samples was investigated by the determination of D-PA in human plasma samples. The real samples were obtained from three healthy people. 500μL sample solution was used to perform based on the procedure described in Section 2.3. The D-PA contents in human plasma samples were derived from the standard curve and the regression equation, average recovery test was performed by using the standard addition method, analytical data were summarized in Table 3 . As we can see from Table 3, the recoveries of this “off-on” sensing system were in the ranged from 98.5% to 102.0%, with RSD less than 2.5%, indicating satisfied recovery and precision of the proposed method. Therefore, the new “off-on” sensing platform provided a simple and convenient way to analysis D-PA directly in human plasma samples.

According to the significant fluorescence enhancement of the CDs-Hg2+ system in the simultaneous presence of D-PA, an “AND” logic gate was designed (Fig. 11 ). In this logic gate, Hg2+ and D-PA were used as inputs along with the fluorescent state of “off” and “on”. (Scheme. 1) and the normalization fluorescence intensity of the system at a wavelength of 442nm as output. For output shown in truth table (Fig. 11), fluorescence recovery and non-fluorescence recovery intensity were defined as “1” and “0”, respectively. For input, the presence of Hg2+ or D-PA was defined as the “1” state and their absence as the “0” state. With no input (0, 0), the output was “0”. Similarly, with Hg2+ (1, 0) or D-PA (0, 1) input alone, the fluorescence intensity was also extremely weak and the output was “0”. When the system was subjected to the two inputs together (1, 1), the fluorescence intensity increased sharply, giving an output signal of “1”. Therefore, a Boolean logic gate was constructed where the gate (output) was only activated when both the inputs were present [61].

In summary, a fluorescence quenching method for the detection of D-Penicillamine (D-PA) using carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probe is established. With the proposed CDs-based fluorescence probe, the prepared sensor exhibit a lower detection limit and wider linear range compared with other most D-PA sensor. Moreover, the system also enabled the design of an “AND” logic gate. With the fluorescence recovery intensity defined as output, only the presence of both Hg2+ and D-PA could make the gate active. In addition, this sensing strategy will open up a new perspective for the design of novel sensors and inspire the development of multifunctional CDs- based platforms with various applications in biosensing, imaging, drug delivery, and so on.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support for this study by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 21575117) and the Special Fund of Chongqing Key Laboratory (CSTC).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltammetric sensor for D-penicillamine determination based on its electrocatalytic oxidation at the surface of ferrocenes modified carbon paste electrodes
Selective determination of penicillamine by on-line vapor-phase generation combined with Fourier transform infrared spectrometry
A novel HPLC-electrochemical detection approach for the determination of D-penicillamine in skin specimens
High performance liquid chromatography analysis of d-penicillamineby derivatizationwith N-(1-pyrenyl)maleimide (NPM)
Determination of thiol by high-performance liquid chromatography and fluorescence detection with 5-methyl-(2-(m-iodoacetylaminophenyl) benzoxazole
Simultaneous determination of tiopronin and D-penicillamine in human urine by liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detection
Use of 4-(6-methylnaphthalen-2-yl)-4-oxobut-2-enoic acid as a reagent for the spectrophotometric and fluorimetric determination of aliphatic thiol drugs
Determination of penicillamine in pharmaceuticals and human plasma by capillary electrophoresis with in-column fiber optics light-emitting diode induced fluorescence detection
A sequential injection spectrophotometric method for the determination of penicillamine in pharmaceutical products by complexation with iron(III) in acidic media
Chemiluminescence determination of penicillamine via flow injection applying a quinine–cerium(IV) system
Plasma D-penicillamine redox state evaluation by capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence
Determination of penicillamine in pharmaceuticals and human plasma by capillary electrophoresis with in-column fiber optics light-emitting diode induced fluorescence detection
Ultraviolet derivatization of low-molecular-mass thiols for high performance liquid chromatography and capillary electrophoresis analysis
Enzymatic oxidation of tert-butylcatechol in the presence of sulfhydryl compounds: application to the amperometric detection of penicillamine
A novel strategy for selective determination of d-penicillamine based on molecularly imprinted polypyrrole electrode via the electrochemical oxidation with ferrocyanide
Voltammetric sensor for D-penicillamine determination based on its electrocatalytic oxidation at the surface of ferrocenes modified carbon paste electrodes
Electrochemical analysis of D-penicillamine using a boron-doped diamond thin film electrode applied to flow injection system

Specific determination of cysteine and penicillamine through cyclization to 2-thioxothiazolidine-4-carboxylic acids
A novel method for detecting allura red based on triple-wavelength overlapping resonance Rayleigh scattering
Bright fluorescent nanodiamonds: no photobleaching and low cytotoxicity
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo
Electrochemiluminescence of water-soluble carbon nanocrystals released electrochemically from graphite
An electrochemical avenue to blue luminescent nanocrystals from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs)
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
Extraction of electrochemiluminescent oxidized carbon quantum dots from activated carbon
Microwave–hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from graphite oxide
Simple aqueous solution route to luminescent carbogenic dots from carbohydrates
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of water-soluble carbon nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescent properties
An aqueous route to multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots using silica spheres as carriers
Semiconductor nanocrystals as fluorescent biological labels
In vivo cancer targeting and imaging with semiconductor quantum dots
DNA hybridization detection with blue luminescent quantum dots and dye-labeled single-stranded DNA
Quantum dot bioconjugates for ultrasensitive nonisotopic detection
Ultrasensitive determination of cysteine based on the photocurrent of nafion-functionalized CdS–MV quantum dots on an ITO electrode
Semiconductor and carbon-based fluorescent nanodots: the need for consistency
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Use of surface-modified CdTe quantum dots as fluorescent probes in sensing mercury(II)
Sensitive and selective sensor for biothiols in the cell based on the recovered fluorescence of the CdTe quantum dots-Hg(II) system
Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions
Fluorescent carbon dots for auramine O determination and logic gate operation
Green synthesis of luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from milk and its imaging application

A selective fluorescence quenching method for the determination of trace hypochlorite in water samples with nile blue A
CdTe quantum dot as a fluorescence probe for vitamin B 12 in dosage form
Determination of nucleic acids based on the fluorescence quenching of Hoechst 33258 at pH 4.5
A new fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dot system modified by the fluorophore-labeled ssDNA for the analysis of 6-mercaptopurine and Hg(II)

A spectrofluorimetric sequential injection method for the determination of penicillamine using fluorescamine in the presence of β-cyclodextrins
A fluorescence detection of D-penicillamine based on Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching system of protein-stabilized gold nanoclusters

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yusheng Yuan A second-grade graduate student of School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Southwest University, 400715, Chongqing, China. Email: 747242223@qq.co.
Xin Zhao A junior undergraduate student of School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Southwest University, 400715, Chongqing, China. Email: 383874328@qq.com.
